verses.text: Chronicles opens not with Israel but with Adam — the whole human race. Before tracing the chosen line, it roots God's people in all mankind. The God of Israel is the God of every nation, and His redemptive plan spans the world from the first man.

The sons of Japheth spread into the distant coastlands and northern peoples. The nations are named with care, not contempt. Scripture honours the wide family of mankind even as it moves toward the one line of promise.

The sons of Japheth spread into the distant coastlands and northern peoples. The nations are named with care, not contempt. Scripture honours the wide family of mankind even as it moves toward the one line of promise.

The sons of Japheth spread into the distant coastlands and northern peoples. The nations are named with care, not contempt. Scripture honours the wide family of mankind even as it moves toward the one line of promise.

Ham's line includes Nimrod, the first mighty empire-builder, and the peoples of Egypt and beyond. Human greatness and earthly power are noted in passing. Empires rise in the genealogies, but the story's true thread runs through a humbler line God has chosen.

Canaan's descendants are listed — the very peoples Israel would later face in the land. The roll quietly marks those who would oppose God's purpose. Even the future adversaries have a place in the record of the God who rules all nations.

Shem's line is traced toward Eber and Peleg, in whose days 'the earth was divided.' The chosen branch begins to narrow. From all the spreading nations, God is singling out the family through whom blessing will come.

Shem's line is traced toward Eber and Peleg, in whose days 'the earth was divided.' The chosen branch begins to narrow. From all the spreading nations, God is singling out the family through whom blessing will come.

Shem's line is traced toward Eber and Peleg, in whose days 'the earth was divided.' The chosen branch begins to narrow. From all the spreading nations, God is singling out the family through whom blessing will come.

The sons of Joktan fill out Shem's wider family. Many branches are named that lead nowhere in the main story. God keeps the full account, even of lines that pass from view, for nothing in His ordering of the nations is forgotten.

The sons of Joktan fill out Shem's wider family. Many branches are named that lead nowhere in the main story. God keeps the full account, even of lines that pass from view, for nothing in His ordering of the nations is forgotten.

The line runs straight from Shem to Abram, 'the same is Abraham.' Ten names carry the promise from the flood to the father of the faithful. God's purpose moves unhurried through the generations toward the man He would call.

Abraham's sons are named, with Ishmael's twelve princes given their due. The son not chosen is still blessed and recorded. God's election of one does not mean abandonment of the other; Ishmael too has his honoured place.

The sons of Keturah, Abraham's other family, are listed and set aside. The branches that are not the line of promise are acknowledged, then left. God narrows His covenant line without despising the rest of the family.

The sons of Keturah, Abraham's other family, are listed and set aside. The branches that are not the line of promise are acknowledged, then left. God narrows His covenant line without despising the rest of the family.

Esau's sons are named before the genealogy returns to Israel. The elder brother's line is recorded fully and with care. Though the blessing passed to Jacob, Esau's descendants are not erased from God's book.

The sons of Seir, the Horites who dwelt in Edom's land, are listed. Even peoples outside the covenant are given a place. The Chronicler's God is no narrow tribal deity but the Lord of all the families of the earth.

Edom had kings 'before any king reigned over Israel.' The brother-nation had its monarchy first. Yet Edom's kingship, glorious for a time, points by contrast to the everlasting throne God would establish through David's line.

The list of Edom's kings continues, each succeeding the last. Their thrones pass quickly from one to another. Earthly dynasties rise and fall in a few verses, while the line God is tracing leads to a kingdom that will never end.

The dukes of Edom close the chapter. The genealogy has swept from Adam through all the nations to Israel's nearest kin. Having set the whole world in view, the record now turns to the people God chose to bless the world.
